votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the recursive Coq function using an equivalent iterative loop, toggling the boolean flag at each step and summing the even elements at odd indices.
The Python code implements the exact same logic as the Coq code, iterating through the list and accumulating even numbers at odd indices.
The Python code correctly implements the recursive logic of the Coq function using an iterative loop, accurately toggling the boolean flag and checking for even numbers.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic: it iterates through the list, toggling is_odd_idx each step, and adds x to the total only when is_odd_idx is True and x is even (x % 2 == 0 matches Z.even). The spec checks if res equals add_sum(lst, False), matching the Coq definition.

Both implementations toggle a boolean per element and sum elements that are even when the toggle is true, starting with false. Python ints and modulo match Coq Z and Z.even for all integers, including negatives.

Both sum even elements at alternating indices starting with skipping the first (is_odd_idx toggles each step), and add_spec checks equality with the same initial flag. Handling of negatives and parity matches (Z.even vs x % 2 == 0).
